situation: A colleague compliments your Chinese ability

options.feedback: Classic Chinese deflection — humble and culturally perfect.
Acceptable — the 还在学习 adds humility, but a direct 谢谢 is Western-style.

options.text: 是的，我花了很多时间学的。
options.feedback: Accepting compliments directly is uncomfortable in Chinese culture.
过奖 (over-praised) — another classic deflection. Very natural.
Opening gifts immediately is Western — in China, open it later.
Ritual refusal then acceptance is the norm. 太客气了 is perfect.
Warm but misses the expected 推辞 (ritual refusal) step.
破费 (you spent money) — acknowledging their expense is polite.

situation: A friend's parent asks your age
situation_zh: 朋友的父母问你多大了

options.feedback: Direct answer is expected — age isn't private in China.
Refusing to share age seems secretive and rude in Chinese culture.
Playful deflection might work with peers, not with elders.
Answering with zodiac animal is very Chinese — they can calculate your age.
Doubled 不好意思 shows sincerity — the go-to for minor incidents.
对不起 works but implies more guilt — 不好意思 is lighter.
Checking if they're OK is thoughtful but misses the apology.
This tells THEM to be careful — blaming the victim. Don't.

situation: Host keeps offering you more food, you're full
situation_zh: 主人一直让你多吃，你吃饱了

options.feedback: Adding 真的 (really) breaks through the ritual offering cycle.
Doubled 够了 is emphatic. Works, but could sound a bit blunt.

options.text: 我吃不下了，做得太好吃了。
options.feedback: 吃不下 + complimenting the food — peak politeness.
Too blunt — hosts will think you didn't enjoy the food.

situation: 方阿姨 looks you up and down and says “最近是不是胖了一点？” (Have you put on weight recently?)
situation_zh: 方阿姨上下打量你，说“最近是不是胖了一点？”

options.text: 哈哈，可能吧，最近吃得太好了。
options.feedback: Self-deprecating humor that accepts the comment without confrontation. Deflects with a light reason. This is the smoothest Chinese response to uncomfortable directness from elders.
Mild denial. Not rude, but 方阿姨 will double down. You haven't defused anything.

options.text: 阿姨，这样说不太礼貌吧。
options.feedback: Calling out an elder's manners directly is a confrontation most Chinese speakers would avoid. You'll win the point and lose the relationship.

options.text: 是吗？那我得少吃点了，阿姨帮我监督。
options.feedback: Accepting + recruiting her as ally is clever. Turns her sharpness into involvement. A bit performative but socially effective.

situation: 老周 enthusiastically tells your mutual friend: “没问题，他肯定愿意帮忙搬家！” (No problem, he'll definitely help with the move!) — without asking you first.
situation_zh: 老周热心地跟朋友说“没问题，他肯定愿意帮忙搬家！”你还没答应过。

options.text: 老周，你太热心了。那天我看看有没有时间吧。
options.feedback: Acknowledges his enthusiasm (face-saving) while creating an exit. 看看有没有时间 is the classic soft maybe-no. Textbook handling.
Directly contradicting 老周 in front of others makes him lose face. Even if true, this burns the bridge.
Going along avoids conflict but you just committed to something you didn't choose. 老周 will keep volunteering you.

options.text: 哈哈老周，你比我还积极。我周末可能有事，我先确认一下。
options.feedback: Humor + vague schedule conflict + promise to confirm. Gives you time and space without anyone losing face.

situation: 秦经理 hands you a three-page form and says “这是流程要求的，麻烦你填一下。” (This is required by the process, please fill it out.) The form is for a request that should take one email.
situation_zh: 秦经理递给你一份三页的表格，说“这是流程要求的，麻烦你填一下。”其实一封邮件就能解决。

options.text: 好的，秦经理。有不清楚的地方我再问您。
options.feedback: Accept the process, signal competence, leave door open for questions. 秦经理 respects compliance and will remember your cooperation.

options.text: 这个有必要吗？发个邮件不就行了？
options.feedback: Questioning a manager's process in front of others challenges their authority. Even if you're right, you lose.
Clean compliance. Slightly less optimal than opening a channel for questions, but perfectly appropriate.

options.text: 秦经理，这个表格和上次的一样吗？我可以参考上次的填吗？
options.feedback: Shows initiative and familiarity with process. Good, but slightly implies you find it repetitive.

situation: 罗老师 clutches his chest and announces: “大事不好了！” (Disaster!) — then reveals the classroom projector bulb needs replacing.
situation_zh: 罗老师捂着胸口喊道“大事不好了！”原来是教室投影仪的灯泡要换了。

options.text: 罗老师，别着急，我去找人来换。
options.feedback: Calm acknowledgment + immediate practical offer. You take his concern seriously without matching his drama. This is exactly how to handle 罗老师.
Dismissing his theatricality directly bruises his ego. 罗老师 thrives on dramatic stakes — telling him to calm down never works.
Matching his panic validates his drama but escalates the situation. You're now both panicking about a light bulb.

options.text: 罗老师放心，这个好解决。我来处理。
options.feedback: 放心 (relax) is gentle, and 我来处理 (I'll handle it) gives him a hero's exit. Very smooth.

situation: 老白 confidently tells you the subway station is to the left. You're fairly sure it's to the right.
situation_zh: 老白很有把握地说地铁站在左边，你比较确定是在右边。

options.text: 是吗？我记得好像是右边，我们查一下地图？
options.feedback: Soft hedge (好像) + external authority (map) means nobody loses face. 老白's confidence stays intact while you get the right answer.
Flat contradiction. 老白 will dig in harder, and now it's a power struggle over a subway station.
Avoiding conflict by going the wrong direction is harmony at the cost of getting lost. Not worth it.

options.text: 老白，你去过那么多地方，不过我上次好像是从右边走的。要不咱们先查查？
options.feedback: Compliment his experience, then offer contradicting evidence softly. The 咱们先查查 frames it as collaboration, not correction.

situation: You ask 小梅 whether the restaurant next door is good. She says: “还行。” (It's okay.) You need more information.
situation_zh: 你问小梅隔壁那家餐厅好不好吃，她只说了“还行。”你需要更多信息。

options.text: 还行是好吃还是不好吃？
options.feedback: Pushing 小梅 to decode her own understatement feels like an interrogation. She said what she said.
Shifting from opinion to specifics. 小梅 won't give you adjectives, but she'll tell you what she orders. This is how you extract information from minimalists.
Accepting her non-answer and moving on. You still don't know anything, but at least you didn't push.

options.text: 他们的什么菜比较有特色？
options.feedback: Good concrete question. 小梅 might answer this because it's factual, not opinion-based. Slightly less natural than asking what she personally orders.

situation: 方阿姨 and 老周 are arguing about where to have the group dinner. 方阿姨 wants the quiet Sichuan place; 老周 insists on the loud hotpot buffet. They both turn to you.
situation_zh: 方阿姨和老周在争论聚餐去哪儿。方阿姨要去安静的川菜馆，老周坚持去热闹的火锅自助。两个人都看着你。

options.text: 两个都不错。要不这次川菜，下次火锅？
options.feedback: The classic Chinese compromise: acknowledge both, sequence them. Everyone gets something, nobody loses face. 方阿姨 wins this round, but 老周 gets a promise.

options.text: 我觉得方阿姨说得对，安静一点好聊天。
options.feedback: Siding with 方阿姨 gives a reason, but 老周 will feel rejected. Taking sides openly in a group dynamic is risky.
我都行 sounds neutral but is actually useless. They asked for your input. Ducking makes you look like you don't care.

options.text: 要不我们问问大家？看多数人想去哪儿。
options.feedback: Deferring to group vote removes you from the crossfire. Smart, but 方阿姨 might read it as you not having the courage to choose.

situation: 秦经理 tells you: “按照规定，优秀员工可以提前半小时下班。你这个月表现不错。” (Per policy, outstanding employees can leave 30 minutes early. You've done well this month.)
situation_zh: 秦经理说“按照规定，优秀员工可以提前半小时下班。你这个月表现不错。”

options.text: 谢谢秦经理，我会继续努力的。
options.feedback: Receive the warmth, reflect it as motivation. You're acknowledging both the policy framing and the personal kindness underneath. Perfect register for a bureaucrat with a heart.

options.text: 真的吗？太好了！那我今天就早走了。
options.feedback: Too eager. 秦经理 offered a privilege, not a holiday. Jumping on it immediately looks like you've been watching the clock.

options.text: 不用了，秦经理，我把手头的事做完再走。
options.feedback: Ritual refusal of a benefit shows dedication. 秦经理 will appreciate it, though she might insist. Either way, you look good.

options.text: 秦经理，这个规定我怎么没听说过？
options.feedback: Questioning the rule misses the point entirely. She's being kind to you through the language of policy. Don't audit her generosity.

situation: You're rushing to catch a bus. 老白 stops you and asks: “你说，人活着到底是为了什么？” (What do you think — what are people really living for?)
situation_zh: 你赶着去坐公交车，老白拦住你问“你说，人活着到底是为了什么？”

options.text: 老白，这个问题太大了，等我回来慢慢聊。我先赶车！
options.feedback: Respects the question's weight (太大了) while creating a clean exit. The promise to return flatters 老白 without committing to philosophy at a bus stop.
Dismissing 老白's question is dismissing 老白. He'll let you go but he'll remember the brush-off.

options.text: 为了赶公交车吧！哈哈。回头聊！
options.feedback: Humor that links the existential to the immediate. 老白 might actually appreciate the irony. Quick and warm.

options.text: 我觉得……就是为了开心吧。
options.feedback: Engaging with the question when you need to leave means you'll miss the bus AND give a shallow answer. 老白 deserves better, and so does your schedule.

situation: 罗老师 stands up at the end of class, gestures broadly, and declares: “今天这篇作文，是我教书二十年来见过最有灵魂的！” (This essay is the most soulful I've seen in twenty years of teaching!) He's talking about your 200-word homework.
situation_zh: 罗老师站起来，大手一挥说“今天这篇作文，是我教书二十年来见过最有灵魂的！”他说的是你两百字的作业。

options.text: 罗老师过奖了，还有很多不足的地方。
options.feedback: Classic deflection that respects his praise without buying into the exaggeration. 过奖 is the right register for a teacher, and naming your own shortcomings shows humility.

options.text: 谢谢罗老师的鼓励，我会继续努力。
options.feedback: Reframing his superlative as 鼓励 (encouragement) gently scales it down. 继续努力 is the eternal student pledge. Very safe, very good.

options.text: 哈哈，罗老师，您太夸张了吧。
options.feedback: Calling out his theatricality directly. It's honest and he might even laugh, but telling a teacher he's exaggerating risks making him feel silly.
Accepting the over-the-top praise at face value makes you look either naive or arrogant. Neither is a good look in a Chinese classroom.
